title: Dye Transfer on Polyester
timeEstimate: 1–6 hour cool soak; speed-critical, polyester locks set dye
stainChemistry: Dye transfer on polyester is bleed from one garment onto another in the wash — typically dark denim, new colored items, or non-colorfast goods. Polyester is dyed industrially with disperse dyes at high temperature, which is exactly the trap on dye transfer: any heat at home (hot wash, hot dryer) drives the transferred dye INTO the polyester fiber the same way intentional dyeing does, and the result is permanent. Polyester also tends to lock set dye more aggressively than cotton because the fiber is hydrophobic and the dye bonds are non-polar. Two hard lines: (1) NEVER chlorine bleach (locks azo dyes into a yellow oxidation product permanently and damages polyester) and (2) NEVER hot water or hot dryer until cleared. White polyester tolerates oxygen bleach + reducing-bleach color removers. Dyed polyester narrows sharply because color removers strip garment dye too.
whyThisWorks: Speed plus oxygen bleach in cool water is the most reliable home approach: re-wash IMMEDIATELY in cool before the dye sets through heat exposure, then OxiClean cool soak to break color bonds. For partially set transfer on white polyester, a reducing-bleach color remover (Carbona / Rit) is the home nuclear option. The honest cap on polyester: once heat-set, it behaves like an intentional dye job and is essentially permanent. Polyester is more punishing than cotton on dye transfer — speed and cool-only discipline matter more.
defaultAssumption: White polyester garment / blend / sportswear + dye transfer from a dark item in a recent wash, fresh and not yet hot-dried. Hot-dried polyester = usually permanent. Dyed polyester = pro is often the right call.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Hot-dried polyester with dye transfer is usually permanent — escalate or accept-as-is. Care label dry-clean-only / specialty performance fabric = pro. Otherwise act fast, do NOT dry the item again.

spottingProtocol.technique: Repeat the wash before any heat exposure
spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, normal detergent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool wash NOW, normal detergent. Do NOT dry. Polyester punishes any heat — keep it strictly cool until cleared.

spottingProtocol.technique: Oxygen bleach to break color bonds
spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean per label, cool water, soak basin
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix OxiClean per label in COOL water. Soak 1–6 hours. Check hourly — pull when transfer clears. Cool rinse, cool re-wash. Repeat soak if partial.

spottingProtocol.agent: Color Remover for White Polyester Only — Nuclear
spottingProtocol.equipment: Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: WHITE polyester only. Per label — Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover (sodium hydrosulfite). Strips ALL dye. Ventilate (sulfur smell). Cool rinse, cool wash. Some Rit formulations call for warm water — stay on the cool end of the label and skip if the label demands hot.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 3% H₂O₂ on damp cloth or as soak additive
spottingProtocol.instruction: WHITE polyester only — hidden test on dyed polyester first. 3% peroxide damp cloth or add to OxiClean soak. Cool rinse.

spottingProtocol.agent: AIR-DRY in Direct Sun — No Heat Until Clear
spottingProtocol.instruction: AIR-DRY in direct sunlight. UV breaks down residual dye. NEVER hot dryer or iron until transfer is fully gone — polyester locks dye under heat permanently. If color remains, repeat OxiClean soak before any heat.

steps: 1. Triage — hot-dried (usually permanent), dry-clean-only / specialty = pro.
3. OxiClean cool soak 1–6 hr, check hourly.
4. Color Remover (reducing bleach) for white polyester only, ventilation.
5. 3% H₂O₂ for residual color on white polyester.
6. AIR-DRY plus sun-dry. NO heat until clear.
products: Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover (white polyester only)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat permanently sets dye into polyester
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — locks dye permanently and damages polyester
NEVER hot water or hot dryer — heat sets dye into polyester forever
Color Remover is white-only — strips all dye including the polyester's
Polyester locks set dye more aggressively than cotton — speed matters
Sulfur smell from reducing bleach — ventilate

homeSolutions.agent: Polyester Punishes Heat on Dye Transfer
homeSolutions.instruction: Polyester is industrially dyed with heat — and the same heat at home drives transferred dye into the fiber permanently. If the item has already been through a hot wash or hot dryer with the transfer on it, the dye is usually set. If not, you have a real shot — but only with strict cool discipline. Two hard rules: NO chlorine bleach (permanent yellow lock) and NO heat until cleared.

Dry-clean-only polyester, specialty performance fabric (Gore-Tex, athletic compression), or dyed colored polyester narrow your home options sharply. White or light washable polyester = home protocol candidate. Anything else = pro.
Run the polyester through a cool wash cycle right now with normal detergent. Do NOT dry. The dye is partially soluble in cool water during the first wash — every hour of delay reduces success.

Mix OxiClean per label in COOL water. Submerge the polyester. Check every hour — many transfers clear within 2 to 3 hours. Cool rinse, second cool wash with detergent. Repeat soak if partial improvement.

homeSolutions.agent: Color Remover — White Polyester Only, Last Resort
homeSolutions.instruction: If white polyester still has color after the OxiClean soak: Carbona Color Run Remover or Rit Color Remover (sodium hydrosulfite). Per label. Ventilate. DO NOT use on dyed polyester — strips ALL dye including the garment's. If the label calls for hot water, skip this step on polyester — hot sets any unrecovered dye permanently.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% Peroxide for Residual Color on White
homeSolutions.instruction: Residual color on WHITE polyester: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, or add to the OxiClean soak. Hidden-area test on dyed polyester first.

homeSolutions.agent: AIR-DRY in Direct Sun — No Heat Until Clear
homeSolutions.instruction: Hang in direct sunlight to dry. UV breaks down residual dye on polyester. NEVER hot dryer or iron until the transfer is fully gone — heat permanently sets dye into polyester. If color remains after sun-dry, repeat OxiClean soak before any heat.

steps: 1. NO chlorine bleach. NO heat until fully clear.
2. Triage — hot-dried = usually permanent. Dry-clean-only / specialty = pro.
4. OxiClean cool soak 1–6 hr, check hourly.
5. Carbona / Rit Color Remover for WHITE polyester only, ventilate.
6. 3% peroxide for residual color on white polyester, hidden test on dyed.
7. AIR-DRY plus sun-dry. NO heat until fully clear.
products: Carbona Color Run Remover / Rit Color Remover (white polyester only)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot-dried (usually permanent). Stop if dyed / specialty performance / dry-clean-only polyester (pro). Stop if Color Remover label requires hot water (would set unrecovered dye). Stop after 2 OxiClean soaks plus Color Remover (if eligible) without progress.
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — permanent lock on polyester
NEVER hot water or hot dryer — permanent dye set
Polyester locks set dye more than cotton — speed matters
Dyed polyester / specialty performance fabric = pro

neverDo: Never chlorine bleach on polyester — damages fiber + permanent dye lock
Never hot water or hot dryer until cleared
Never Color Remover on dyed polyester
Never iron the stained area before clearing
fiberSensitivities: White polyester: tolerates OxiClean cool soak, Color Remover (white only), 3% peroxide
Dyed polyester: hidden test mandatory before peroxide; Color Remover banned
Performance polyester (athletic, technical): pro — coatings react unpredictably
Polyester is hydrophobic — set dye locks more aggressively than on cotton
Polyester yellows under heat — additional reason no hot dryer

materialWarnings: Speed matters more than any product choice
No heat until fully clear — polyester locks dye under heat
Performance / specialty polyester = pro from minute one

escalation.when: Hot dryer already used (usually permanent). Dyed polyester. Performance / dry-clean-only / specialty polyester. 2 OxiClean soaks plus eligible Color Remover plateau. Active dye spread continuing during home treatment.
escalation.whatToTell: "Dye transfer on [white / dyed] polyester, [fresh / hot-dried], source [denim / red garment / mixed wash], home-treated with [cool re-wash + OxiClean cool soak × N + Color Remover on white + 3% peroxide on white + sun-dry]. No chlorine bleach, no heat. [Residual color / hot-dried suspected / spreading]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with reducing-bleach chemistry (sodium hydrosulfite controlled dilution) and polyester color-stripping experience

contextQuestions: How fresh — current wash or hot-dried already?
Source of the transfer — denim / red / mixed?
Care label — washable, dry-clean-only, performance, specialty?
Has the item been ironed since the transfer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fresh + white + not hot-dried = real shot at home recovery. Hot-dried = usually permanent. Dyed polyester = sharply narrowed bench. Performance / technical polyester = pro from minute one because the fabric coatings react unpredictably to oxygen bleach and reducing bleach. Red and navy transfers are the toughest — budget extra OxiClean cycles.
